data.title: Are The Stars Aligning For A New Bull Run In Homebuilder Stocks?
data.description:
The relatively weak performance of shares in the homebuilder industry for the past two calendar years is, on first glance, surprising. A long-running under-investment in home building has created a housing shortage at a time of a demographic-driven rise in demand. But holding an ETF that tracks shares in the homebuilding industry has only matched the return of the broad equity market over the past five years but at a cost of sharply higher volatility.

It’s timely to wonder if the fortunes of homebuilder stocks are poised to improve. One catalyst for change is the slide in borrowing costs. The average long-term US mortgage rate, based on the 30-year fixed rate, fell to 6.16% last week, the lowest in over three years, according to mortgage buyer Freddie Mac.

Softer borrowing costs should be a net plus at a time when, according to a recent Goldman Sachs estimate: “At least 3-4 million additional homes beyond normal construction need to be built to address the shortage in US housing supply and boost affordability.”

Despite the supply-demand tailwind, several factors have slowed the industry, including a decline in housing affordability that’s accelerated since the pandemic and various land-use restrictions, Goldman analysts note. Last week’s news that US housing starts in October fell to lowest level in nearly six years reminds that a quick fix doesn’t look imminent.

But the ongoing supply shortage, in time, will likely create conditions for a stronger run in homebuilder stocks. Politics may be a supporting factor too. The White House in recent weeks has reportedly met with industrial officials on ways to improve affordability and how the administration could help.

In a possible early hint of things to come, market sentiment has turned firmly bullish on homebuilders this year. The S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB) has rallied more than 12% year to date through Thursday’s close (Jan. 15), far above the broad stock market’s 1.5% gain, based on the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

Encouraging, but a couple of weeks could be noise. Meanwhile, homebuilders are still far behind equities overall for the past several years. For the trailing one-year window, for instance, SPY is handily outperforming XHB: 18.2% vs. 6.4%.

If the tide begins turning in favor of homebuilders, the investment case will look more convincing in relative terms against stocks generally. One way to monitor the trend is by tracking the ratio of XHB to SPY.

Despite the recent pop in homebuilder shares, it’s not clear that the industry’s relatively weak performance since 2024 has run its course. A stronger sign that the cycle is turning would arise when the 50-day average for this ratio recovers and rises above the 200-day average. On that basis, the jury’s still out if homebuilders are poised to outshine the broad market in 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BigBear.ai: Ratings Upgrade; Still Speculative, But Worth Some Exposure
data.description:
BigBear.ai (BBAI) has been on the volatile side of trading action over the past year, with the 52 week range standing at ~$2.40-$9.80. There has been a lot of money made and lost in this name but one thing holds true, this remains a speculative play.

BBAI is a software company that specializes in AI, machine learning, and data analytics. A lot of buzzwords, but the company provides decision intelligence and predictive analytics tools for enterprises and government agencies, primarily targeting national security, defense, and critical infrastructure use-cases.

Having "AI" in your company name, in many cases, has driven high levels of investor interest over the past year. With AI carrying a positive sentiment, both from an investing and momentum standpoint, BBAI has been at the center of action. On top of that, given the company's exposure to U.S. Government spending, positive momentum around the defense budget should be a net tailwind for BBAI.

I was bearish on BBAI several months ago, however, given the lack of stock action despite AI remaining a sought after exposure, I believe BBAI is in a much better position. While operationally, not much has changed over the past 1-2 quarters, it appears investor sentiment is more normalized and the backdrop around potential future contracts seems stronger than ever.

Yes, there is still some hype in the stock price and it is challenge to academically value this company based on its current financial set-up. However, the buying environment around AI solutions, notably at the U.S. Government level, is a multi-year tailwind. One only needs to look at the growth, hype, and expectations around Palantir (PLTR) to see how much optimism and actual spending is occurring in this end-market.

While the stock is not a clear buy for everyone, I believe BBAI now falls into the speculative buy category for those looking to get Palantir-like exposure.

Improving Fundamental Backdrop

The comparison with Palantir is not exactly apples-to-apples. Although both operate under the broader umbrella of government agencies, Palantir is doing so at a much larger, more established scale. Depending on the use-case, there can be some competitive overlap. That being said, BBAI's exposure to the government sector is a real driver of its business, and represents ~1/3 of its total revenue.

2025 was a challenging year for BBAI, who saw several contract delays given the prolonged government shutdown. This affected several software vendors serving the public sector, so it is not a BBAI-specific issue.

Fast forward to today, the government budget is very strong, with an emphasis on digital/AI investments. In fact, the U.S. Federal government requested $13.4 billion for AI and autonomy investments in 2026.

Using the chart above, the key area for BBAI is the $1.2 billion in software and cross-domain integration and the $200 million in AI and automation technology. Of course, just because this is in the budget does not mean that BBAI will win any of these contracts. But one thing is clear. The government is keen on investing in AI technologies, and this could be a natural multi-year tailwind for companies such a as BBAI.

BBAI has already achieved several accomplishments across the defense departments. This includes passenger biometric technology at major domestic/international ports of entry, AI orchestration for U.S. Army drone swarm operations, and a strategic collaboration with DEFCON AI.

While new contract wins are not guaranteed, I am a fan of BBAI's positioning.

The company's strength also extends beyond government-related work. In just recent months, the company has announced work with Kraft Group to strengthen supply chain transparency, signed an MOU to advance Pahang Aerospace City development, and implemented biometric screening systems at O'Hare International Airport.

In my opinion, BBAI has done a great job maintaining a diverse business while at the same time doubling-down in areas that have proven to be successful. 2025 was a challenging year, but the prospects look bright for 2026.

Ask Sage Acquisition is Attractive

During the company's last earnings report, they disclosed the acquisition of Ask Sage, "a Generative AI platform for secure distribution of AI models and agentic AI capabilities, built specifically for defense and national security agencies and other highly-regulated sectors." Although the purchase price of $250 million seems a bit pricey considering BBAI's financial standing, I believe this will prove to pay major dividends longer-term. Management talked to some of the expected benefits of Ask Sage on the earnings call.

Ask Sage already supports more than 16,000 government teams across 27 agencies and hundreds of commercial companies and annual recurring revenue is on track to grow over 6x from 2024 to 2025. We will dig deeper into the investment rationale in a few moments, but this is a big development that we expect to accelerate our growth and further advance our position as a technology catalyst for both government and commercial customers.

Ask Sage is expected to deliver $25 million in ARR during 2025, placing the M&A multiple ~10x. However, its revenue grew over 6x during the year, and now that this asset is combined with BBAI's scale, I believe there will be significant amount of synergies to be realized. BBAI will be able to immediately start selling Ask Sage into its existing government contracts. In addition, having the Ask Sage technology may also enable BBAI to win deals they otherwise might not have competed for.

From a balance sheet perspective, this acquisition will make a noticeable impact, though I believe it is manageable.

At the end of Q3, BBAI had $457 million in cash compared to $142 million in debt. However, in early January, the company fully converted its 2029 convertible notes, resulting in $125 million of debt reduction. Of course, this comes at the expense of share dilution, which is something that should be watched. With the stock still up 5% YTD, it seems that investors are more open to share dilution versus a levered balance sheet.

Valuation

Valuation is always tricky with AI-exposed names. Of course, fundamentals are important, but growth stories often require a bit of belief, which embeds some optimism into the multiple.

As mentioned earlier, PLTR is not the best pure-play valuation comp. However, it does signal that investors are willing to pay much higher multiples for a faster-growth, larger company that also has exposure to the government end-market. Should BBAI trade anywhere near PLTR's multiple. Absolutely not. And it does not need to for the stock to work.

The biggest determining factor for BBAI's success will be how much revenue can scale in 2026 and 2027. After a challenging period in 2025 which saw revenue decline due to the U.S. Army contract going away, investors will 100% need to see a strong period of growth.

If there are any signs of estimates moving higher, I believe the valuation multiple will at least maintain its current level, if not move higher.

Risks

Ultimately, the biggest risk to my bullish thesis is that BBAI either loses contracts or fails to win meaningful deals. The U.S. Army contract is a great example - BBAI lost this contract and revenue declined for the year. Losing momentum in the government end-market would be a significant challenge to overcome, and could drive some investors out of the stock.

Additionally, I expect competition to increase. The elephant in the room in Palantir, but there are several AI start-ups that are looking to displace legacy government technology. With the federal government dedicating a large portion of its budget to AI technologies, there will be a lot of greenfield opportunities for many companies to compete.

Finally, while profitability should not be a big focus right now, the company will ultimately need to show signs of economies of scale. Right now, the company has cleaned up its balance sheet, though at the cost of share dilution. Given the company is in growth-mode, this is acceptable. But once growth slows down, the focus will move towards profitability and normalized share growth.

Final Thoughts

I believe the combination of strong U.S. government spending, especially around AI technologies, combined with the recent Ask Sage acquisition put BBAI in a great position to see meaningful revenue acceleration.

Despite a premium valuation, there are several bullish tailwinds that if come to fruition, the stock can really explode from current levels. Yes, this remains somewhat of a speculative play, but I believe proof points in recent months could be an early signal of better things to come.

data.title: Galaxy Digital's Rally Reflects Crypto Activity, Not A Stabilized Earnings Base
data.description:
Galaxy Digital has rallied alongside the broader crypto complex, but a closer, numbers-first review suggests the move reflects improving market conditions rather than the emergence of a stabilized earnings base. Company disclosures and third-party reporting show that profitability remains closely tied to trading activity, balance-sheet revaluations, and liquidity conditions, while Galaxy’s most important diversification effort will not contribute meaningfully to results until at least mid-2026.

Q3 2025 results show scale, but earnings quality remains mixed

Galaxy’s most recent full quarterly disclosure, covering the third quarter of 2025, reported net income of approximately $505 million and adjusted EBITDA of roughly $629 million. At quarter-end, total equity stood at about $3.2 billion, while cash and stablecoins totaled approximately $1.9 billion.

While the headline profitability was striking, management explicitly stated that results reflected a combination of record operating performance in its Digital Assets business and gains on digital asset holdings and investment positions carried on the balance sheet. In other words, reported earnings blended operating income with fair-value remeasurement. That distinction matters, because balance-sheet gains are inherently sensitive to market conditions and may not recur from quarter to quarter.

Trading volumes and lending highlight operating leverage to sentiment

Operating performance in Q3 improved materially, but the drivers were highly conditional. Galaxy reported a sharp increase in spot and derivatives trading volumes, supported in part by the execution of a very large bitcoin transaction for a client, involving more than 80,000 BTC in notional terms.

At the same time, Galaxy disclosed that its average loan book expanded to approximately $1.8 billion during the quarter, supporting higher interest income. Financing and lending revenues tend to scale quickly when crypto liquidity improves and risk appetite rises. They also contract rapidly when volatility subsides or market sentiment turns defensive.

Mark-to-market exposure complicates valuation

A central analytical issue for Galaxy is the extent to which reported earnings are influenced by mark-to-market revaluation. The company holds digital assets and equity investments on its balance sheet, and changes in their fair value flow directly through earnings.

Galaxy explicitly addresses this risk in its SEC filings, noting that results are materially affected by changes in digital asset prices, volatility, and market liquidity. This structure amplifies upside during rallies and downside during drawdowns, making quarter-to-quarter comparisons less informative than for traditional financial firms.

As a result, valuation multiples derived from unusually strong quarters provide limited insight into through-cycle profitability. The stock’s sensitivity to market conditions is a feature of the business model, not a temporary anomaly.

Asset management and staking are growing, but not yet stabilizing

Galaxy has positioned asset management and staking as longer-term stabilizers. As of September 30, 2025, the company reported approximately $17 billion of assets on platform, including about $8.8 billion of assets under management and $6.6 billion of assets under stake, according to its investor overview.

While this represents meaningful scale within the crypto ecosystem, the economics remain constrained. Fee margins in crypto asset management are structurally lower than in traditional alternatives, and staking yields fluctuate with protocol incentives and network conditions. Galaxy’s disclosures show that the contribution from these activities remains modest relative to Global Markets, limiting their ability to anchor consolidated earnings during periods of weaker trading activity.

Helios data center offers scale, but earnings remain deferred

The Helios data center project in West Texas represents Galaxy’s most significant attempt to diversify away from crypto market cycles. Galaxy has disclosed that the site has 800 megawatts of approved power capacity and that CoreWeave has committed to the full amount through executed lease phases.

To fund Phase I development, Galaxy arranged a $1.4 billion project financing facility and separately completed an equity raise with net proceeds of approximately $325 million, materially reducing near-term funding risk.

Despite this progress, management has reiterated that data-center adjusted gross profit and EBITDA are expected to be immaterial until the first half of 2026, when IT capacity delivery and revenue recognition are scheduled to begin. Until then, Helios represents future contracted revenue potential rather than a current contributor to earnings.

GalaxyOne expands distribution, but economics remain opaque

In October 2025, Galaxy launched GalaxyOne, a consumer-facing platform offering access to crypto, equities, and yield products. The move has been framed as an attempt to broaden distribution and compete more directly with established retail platforms. The launch places the firm in more direct competition with consumer-focused brokers and crypto exchanges, marking a strategic shift toward retail distribution.

From an analytical perspective, however, the economics remain opaque. Galaxy has not disclosed customer acquisition costs, active user counts, revenue per user, or contribution margins. Absent these metrics, GalaxyOne is best viewed as a strategic option rather than a near-term earnings driver.

Market expectations embedded in the stock

At prices in the mid-$20s, Galaxy appears to be priced for continued strength in crypto trading activity alongside partial future value from the Helios data center beginning in 2026.

That expectation is also visible in the way Galaxy screens across valuation metrics. On sales-based measures, the stock appears exceptionally cheap, trading at roughly 0.15x trailing sales and about 0.08x forward sales, with EV/Sales at approximately 0.37x trailing and 0.14x forward versus sector medians above 3x. By contrast, earnings-based multiples imply much higher expectations: forward non-GAAP P/E sits around 34x versus a sector median near 11x, while forward EV/EBITDA is roughly 20x compared with about 12x for peers. Price-to-book is similarly elevated at roughly 2.6x trailing, reflecting optimism around balance-sheet growth rather than stabilized cash generation.

What the disclosed numbers do not yet support is a transition to fee-dominated, through-cycle profitability. Asset management remains too small to anchor results, Helios is still pre-revenue, and GalaxyOne is unproven, leaving valuation tightly linked to crypto momentum.

Conclusion

Company disclosures and third-party reporting converge on a consistent conclusion. Galaxy Digital remains primarily an activity-driven, balance-sheet-sensitive business whose earnings rise and fall with crypto market conditions. The company is moving toward a barbell structure that combines cyclical trading and financing with contracted data-center cash flows, but that transition is not yet complete. The decisive inflection point remains the start of Helios revenue recognition in the first half of 2026. Until then, Galaxy’s results should be interpreted as leveraged exposure to crypto activity rather than evidence of a stabilized financial or infrastructure model.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: My Top AI Rebound Pick For 2026
data.description:
My Long Thesis About Super Micro Computer

Super Micro (SMCI) has had its fair share of troubles in 2025, but 2026 presents a new opportunity for the business as AI-led server demand in all likelihood will continue to grow.

Super Micro is growing on the back of significant IT investments, especially in the liquid-cooled portion of the data center market, and I view the business as fundamentally underpriced: with a 10 FWD P/E and sustained growth in its core business, Super Micro is my top investment play for 2026.

Maybe most important of all, after an almost 50% price crash since October, the investment backdrop is quite bullish, in my view, leading to a strong risk/reward setup.

Strong Tailwinds For Liquid-Cooled Server Businesses

Super Micro is providing other companies with hardware solutions in the server realm, especially liquid-cooled servers that are deployed in data centers to run LLMs. These LLMs require considerable compute power and therefore can only be used to the fullest of their potential if they can tap into the most up-to-date GPUs.

Super Micro is a key player in the server market and is a Preferred Partner of NVIDIA (NVDA) which ensures that the company can offer its own clients the newest Graphics Processing Units.

The infrastructure spending tracker from IDC presents a bullish long-term view on AI infrastructure spending (42% implied 5-year CAGR report as per the report) which points us to considerable growth upside for Super Micro in the next couple of years.

Matter-of-factly, IDC’s estimated spending growth will be driven primarily by the kind of accelerated computing architecture that Super Micro is specializing in. This accelerating computing stack revolves around the deployment of AI-optimized GPUs, TPUs, and high-performance clusters that are needed as a core backbone for LLMs.

More precisely, Super Micro is growing with the liquid cooled data center market, a segment that is growing approximately 20% annually. Just in December, Super Micro presented data centers with the opportunity to buy its liquid cooled NVIDIA HGX B300 systems, which are meant for LLM performance enhancements and energy efficiency.

This availability could be a catalyst for stronger growth in 2026, as projections for AI hardware and server spending don’t indicate a cool-down in capex spending in the industry.

Eyes On Super Micro’s Gross Profit Development In 2026

Business and industry drivers are beneficial to Super Micro, but there are some challenges as well. For instance, delays in customer purchases have led to a negative net revenue growth rate in Q1 2026. Total revenues were $5.0 billion, down 15% YoY, but the business should be able to grow its revenues again on the deployment of NVIDIA’s B300 systems.

Maybe most importantly, Super Micro’s gross profits have shrunk as a result, partially due to lower margins in its main server business which tends to be competitive.

If Super Micro can sell a significant amount of high-margin B300 systems through to operators in 2026, there could be a possibility of a margin rebound for the business as well. Of course, a gross profit margin turnaround could be a revitalizing event for Super Micro’s devastated shares.

Probably the weakest point for Super Micro is its margin setup. As a high-volume seller of server systems, Super Micro’s margins are under pressure from falling margins which very well be the business’s biggest vulnerability. In Q1 2026 Super Micro’s adjusted gross margin slid to 9.5%, down 3.6-ppts YoY.

However, with new AI server systems getting ready to be shipped to operators in 2026, my opinion on Super Micro is that the business has a reasonable chance of seeing a margin turnaround in 2026, especially if the company has a good sell-through rate for its B300 systems.

50% Correction Creates Opportunity

Per Tradingview, Super Micro’s share chart shows a considerable loss of investor trust since the November earnings report. Since then, Super Micro has suffered an almost 50% valuation collapse, with shares now being the cheapest since May 2025.

The key reason for the correction is market concerns about Super Micro’s margin trend, which, as I said above, could improve in 2026 if the business sells more higher-margin server systems to data center operators.

Super Micro currently has a Price-To-Earnings Ratio of 9.5 vs. 24.0 for NVIDIA and 8.4 for memory and storage business Micron (MU). The FWD multiples for all companies relate to their next full fiscal years, which in SMCI’s case ends in June 2027.

Super Micro’s margin decline in the last quarter significantly contributed to the business’s significant earnings multiple contraction, but it also creates an investment opportunity for those of us that believe a margin correction to the upside is possible in 2026.

Market dynamics and trends advantage Super Micro’s margin recovery and expanding the FWD earnings multiple to 15.0 (=Fair Value Estimate of $45 a share), to provide a reference point, is easily possible, in my opinion, if margins indeed turn around.

Last year, Super Micro’s FWD P/E ratio was 20.0, and occasionally even higher than that, which suggests a 15x earnings multiple may be a moderate valuation target for SMCI.

Margin Risks And AI Dependency Are Risks

Super Micro’s margins: the business is reeling from falling margins, on both a GAAP and non-GAAP level, amid growing competition for liquid-cooled server racks and other hardware products. Hardware products tend to have small margins, and competition is high, so SMCI faces earnings risks despite an overall market expansion, even in 2026.

Dependence on AI-related spending: Super Micro should be able to grow its net revenue and earnings as long as hyperscalers are willing to spend handsome amounts of money on growing their data center operations. A slowdown in AI-fueled expansion would likely be a signal for continuing margin pressure in 2026 and may prevent a full stock reversal.

My Verdict: Strong Buy

Super Micro has fallen from grace since November 2025, when the business reported a QoQ contraction in net revenue and disclosed continuing margin pressure. However, I believe we have now reached a point where it could make sense to start a small position in the server business.

Super Micro is cheap based on FWD earnings, and though gross margins are a totally legitimate concern right now, Super Micro is profitable, and the chart looks very appealing to me too.

Also, Super Micro’s net revenues should trend up long-term given discussed market dynamics and new system releases, and long-term IDC projections are painting a bullish picture on AI-infrastructure spending. The market seems to discount this potential right now and appears to have been captured by excessively bearish sentiment.

SMCI now accounts for 10% of my portfolio, and I would be happy to increase my exposure to this hardware business on successive dips. Though the margin development should be very carefully watched in the future, the risk/reward ratio appears to be favorable, especially in the context of a 50% correction for Super Micro’s shares since November.

data.title: 8% Vs. 4% Rule: Is Dave Ramsey's Retirement Math Dangerous?
data.description:
4% Vs 8%?

In the past few years, the internet has been abuzz in the financial planning community regarding financial wellness and planning guru Dave Ramsey's vaunted 8% proposed withdrawal rate. He advises on several clips of his show that if you have a mix of broad-based mix of (VOO)(IVV)(DIA)(QQQ) equity mutual funds, you can get a 12% return. This is a 100% equity portfolio, mind you, in theory, as this point he has not been clarified any further. Ramsey states that beating the market is easy with his asset allocation. You get 12% per year, take out 8%, and leave 4% to keep compounding.

While I don't agree that it's easy to beat the market, nor do I agree that 8% will not be destructive in the event of a sequence of negative returns at the outset of retirement, I just don't think everyone is on the same page. You see, when advisors and planners refer to the 4% rule, I'm not sure they understand how that is calculated. 8% of your portfolio could be withdrawn per year, but are we talking about your portfolio or an inflation-adjusted income that compounds over time?

The goal of this article is to parse through the often misunderstood withdrawal rate methodologies and demonstrate some of these sequences using a Monte Carlo simulator.

What is a Monte Carlo simulation?

If you're deep in the weeds of portfolio management or have a financial advisor you may be familiar with or have heard of this software once or twice. For those unfamiliar, it's basically a simulator that looks at 10,000 hypothetical lifetimes through various market scenarios. Its goal is to show you how sustainable your withdrawals are over a number of years, usually 30,40 or even 50. You set a withdrawal amount from your portfolio, the asset allocation, and the withdrawal methodology. More equities typically lead to better long-term results but also increased volatility.

Ray Dalio became a famous hedge fund manager for institutional money for a reason: he focuses on risk-adjusted return, not total return. Standard deviation is the typical measure of volatility and or Beta, depending on what we're trying to measure. Sanity has a dollar value too, and seeing one's capital vacillate 15-25% or more around its mean return could lead some to make irrational, destructive decisions.

Where did the 4% rule come from

The rule is popularized by financial advisor and writer Bill Bengen, based on prior work by Trinity University called the 'Trinity Study'. The study originally determined a safe withdrawal rate unlikely to exhaust a portfolio in the 3-4% range based on a conservative, near half equity and half bond portfolio. Bengen, in his new book titled A Richer Retirement, determined that a safe withdrawal rate with more equities and more asset classes is closer to 5% than 4 [4.7% to be exact]. This is still not a 100% equity portfolio, as he explores up to 70-75% max equity in some interviews. He calls this the 'Safe Max'. The question is, how was this calculated? Let's explore the 3 methods discussed in the book.

The fixed percentage method

This first method is the one Dave Ramsey is most likely referring to. In this one, you take a fixed percentage of your portfolio each year. If it goes up, you take more; if it goes down, you take less. The entire premise is based on the pile of capital you have.

This would be an example of what that would look like at a fixed 5% withdrawal rate:

Year Starting Portfolio Market Return Portfolio Before Withdrawal 5% Withdrawal Ending Portfolio
1 $1,000,000 +8% $1,080,000 $54,000 $1,026,000
2 $1,026,000 -12% $902,880 $45,144 $857,736
3 $857,736 +15% $986,396 $49,320 $937,076
4 $937,076 +5% $983,930 $49,197 $934,733
5 $934,733 -10% $841,260 $42,063 $799,197

If Ramsey is just taking a fixed percentage, it's possibly impossible to run out. Bengen also discusses this in his book because you are always reducing your payout to match your portfolio value. 8% of a dollar is still only 8 cents; you're still drawing 8% even though your portfolio was possibly blown to smithereens.

The fixed annuity method

The fixed annuity method actually turns out to be the most conservative of all the safe withdrawal methods and draws less lifetime capital versus inflation-adjusted, despite being able to take even more at the outset. This fixed annuity method sets a fixed dollar amount, like an annuity, and then draws the same dollar amount every year in perpetuity. Here's an example of a 9% starting withdrawal rate on a $1 million portfolio:

Year Starting Portfolio Market Return Portfolio Before Withdrawal Fixed Withdrawal Ending Portfolio
1 $1,000,000 +8% $1,080,000 $90,000 $990,000
2 $990,000 -12% $871,200 $90,000 $781,200
3 $781,200 +15% $898,380 $90,000 $808,380
4 $808,380 +5% $848,799 $90,000 $758,799
5 $758,799 -10% $682,919 $90,000 $592,919

While the starting 5 years may appear bad, as the portfolio rebounds, compounds and recovers, this $90,000 eventually becomes a smaller and smaller part of the overall capital. Here's a hypothetical years 6-10:

Year Starting Portfolio Market Return Portfolio Before Withdrawal Fixed Withdrawal Ending Portfolio
6 $592,919 +18% $699,644 $90,000 $609,644
7 $609,644 +12% $682,801 $90,000 $592,801
8 $592,801 +10% $652,081 $90,000 $562,081
9 $562,081 +14% $640,772 $90,000 $550,772
10 $550,772 +8% $594,834 $90,000 $504,834

The first 5 years were a bad sequence, with only 1 double-digit up year and 2 double-digit down years. The next 5 years were a minor bull run that still sustained the portfolio through year 10, despite the starting 9% annuity. While 9% is high and unlikely to be the starting point for a retiree, it might be a nice 10-year stop gap, say between 55 years old and 65 with a $1 million taxable brokerage and social security stepping in early as a backstop.

The inflation-adjusted method

Finally, we come to the inflation-adjusted method as described in the Trinity study and Bengen's work. This, in my opinion, is a point that critics of Ramsey fail to point out. The inflation adjusted method says a retiree retires, sets up a withdrawal of 4%, call it $40,000 in this case, on $1 million, and then increases that payout each year by the rate of inflation. It starts at 4% but then compounds with inflation, assuming the retiree needs that much more money each year to sustain their lifestyle in the face of inflation or even deflation. The withdrawal sequence may look something like this:

Year Starting Portfolio Market Return Portfolio Before Withdrawal Inflation Rate Inflation-Adjusted Withdrawal Ending Portfolio
1 $1,000,000 +8% $1,080,000 $40,000 $1,040,000
2 $1,040,000 -12% $915,200 6% $42,400 $872,800
3 $872,800 +15% $1,003,720 7% $45,368 $958,352
4 $958,352 +5% $1,006,270 5% $47,636 $958,634
5 $958,634 -10% $862,771 4% $49,541 $813,230

The studies point out that the most injurious years are those with both negative stock market returns and large amounts of inflation. This forces the retiree to increase their inflation-adjusted withdrawal rate by large percentages while feeding off a shrinking nest egg. This was akin to the 1960s and 70s, one of the worst periods for retirees. Deflation in the studies also causes a reduction in spending, helping out great depression era retirees.

What portfolio Ramsey subscribes to

Ramsey usually mentions a few types of mutual funds, but no specific tickers. These would be :

  • Large-cap growth and income
  • Growth [blend]
  • Agressive growth
  • International

For simple demonstration purposes, I've chosen 4 familiar ETFs to match this allocation. They need to be at least a decade old to accurately back-test and run Monte Carlo simulations on. These would be the following:

  • Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ)- aggressive growth
  • Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO)- growth/blend
  • Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) - growth and income
  • iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA)-international

The portfolio is 100% equity [as Ramsey has spoken about] and split 25% a piece and rebalanced annually.

This million dollar portfolio was set to an 8% withdrawal rate, not an inflation adjusted dollar amount:

The simulated portfolio ended with positive outcomes in each percentile category:

But this would have been very lumpy along the way

From 2012 until current, here was what a backtest of this portfolio would have looked like:

For starters, this portfolio did not match the S&P 500 CAGR; maybe my selections are worse than Ramsey's mutual funds, but they are almost certainly lower cost [just the nature of ETFs VS mutual funds]. Second, the worst year was -17.09%, so one would have had to decrease their income by that amount that year in this scenario. In this case, if you were accustomed to an $80,000 income, you would need to reduce that to $66,328.

Summary

Is this income reduction and increase doable? That's hard to say and really depends on one's individual circumstances. The more fixed costs you have versus variable costs [like a mortgage and a car payment], the less possible this becomes. Ramsey, after all, teaches Christian values with being debt-free at the core of his ethos. If you did own everything free and clear, I suppose a lumpy feast and famine lifestyle could work.

This also assumes a very unique personality type. This individual would have to be very aggressive from a risk perspective [100% equities] and would also need to have spending controls and an aversion to leverage built in. These two personality traits I would assume to be divergent, but exist in some, I have no doubt. This would not be an easy recommendation for the masses.

To sum it up, Dave Ramsey might be right, but I think his critics are also conflating two different withdrawal styles that are not the same. Confusion should be put to rest.

data.title: Acomo: A Much Better Performance Than I Had Anticipated
data.description:
Introduction

When I published my previous article on Acomo (ACNFF) in 2024, I wasn’t sure if the company had turned the corner yet. Perhaps I was a bit too cautious, as Acomo’s performance was indeed on the mend and H2 2024 was exceptionally strong, and that strength continued into 2025.

The share price reacted well thanks to a record performance in H1 2025 while Q3 2025 remained strong, but as Acomo is operating in the volatile business of sourcing, trading, processing and distributing plant-based food ingredients, we have to be mindful of fluctuating margins. This article is meant as an update to previous coverage on Acomo, which you can find here.

Acomo's main listing is on Euronext Amsterdam, where it is trading with ACOMO as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume in Amsterdam is approximately 17,500 shares per day, which represents approximately 310,000 EUR per day, and that makes the Dutch listing clearly superior in terms of volume.

A surprisingly strong 2025

Before discussing the Q3 trading update and a new acquisition that was completed in Q4 2025, I wanted to take a step back and have a look at the company’s results in the first semester of last year.

The total revenue increased by just over 13% to 758M EUR, and although the COGS also increased, that happened at a lower pace which resulted in a very substantial increase in the gross profit result, which jumped by approximately 45%. And despite higher G&A expenses, the operating income almost doubled, to in excess of 63M EUR.

It goes without saying that had a very positive impact on the bottom line result, and Acomo reported a net profit of 42.5M EUR in H1 2025, of which 42.1M EUR was attributable to its common shareholders. That resulted in an EPS of 1.42 EUR. That’s more than twice the 0.61 EUR in earnings per share generated in the first half of 2024. The adjusted net income was slightly lower at 1.36 EUR per share as the FX and CX contribution was negatively impacted by amortization charges and the tax impact on these adjusting items.

As you may remember from my previous article, I judged Acomo based on its cash flow profile, and although the reported operating cash flow was negative to the tune of 28.4M EUR (shown below), keep in mind this included a 87M EUR working capital change (with a substantial portion of that number related to higher inventory levels). It also underestimates the cash interest payments and tax payments by approximately 9M EUR, and after also deducting the 2.5M EUR in lease payments, the adjusted operating cash flow before changes in the working capital position was approximately 47M EUR.

The total capex was just 5.6M EUR, resulting in a net free cash flow of approximately 40M EUR, which is just slightly below the reported net income (as some of the elements that contributed to the higher net income were non-cash items).

This trend continued in the third quarter of 2025, as the company was able to publish a 6% revenue increase. While Acomo does not release detailed financial statements, it is clear we should expect Q3 to be good, considering the comparable quarter a year earlier was already substantially stronger as well.

At the same time, Acomo warned that the growth pace in Q4 will be a bit more subdued, once again due to a very strong performance in the comparable quarter. Additionally, there are some headwinds in the USA as tariffs and unfavorable prices are working against the company.

A new acquisition

Acomo also continues to look for bolt-on M&A ideas, and in Q4 last year, it announced it has entered into an agreement with Hyle Capital Partners to acquire Manuzzi, an Italian company focusing on nuts and dried fruits. The company operates under its own name but also provides white label options for private brands.

The company sees this acquisition as an opportunistic way to increase its exposure to the Southern European market, and as the current Manuzzi management team will stay on, there should be an immediate impact on Acomo’s financial results.

The transaction has already closed and although Acomo has not provided any details on the purchase price and the anticipated EBITDA projections (or the projected synergy benefits), I hope to be able to distill it from the 2026 guidance (when that gets published) while the Q4 cash flow statement should include the acquisition cost.

Investment thesis

While the performance in 2025 remained strong, new headwinds could have a negative impact on the financial performance, and perhaps we should expect the financial performance to remain flat in the next few years. I expect the EPS and FCFPS to come in at around 2.2-2.35 EUR per share in 2025 and assuming a flat performance in the next few years, the company is still attractively priced at about 10-11 times earnings and a free cash flow yield of around 9-11%. This also means the generous dividend (1.25 EUR per share for FY 2024) remains very well covered while the debt ratio should remain stable at around 2x EBITDA (and decreasing, as the company monetizes working capital elements, and as some of its earnings are retained on the balance sheet).

I currently have no position in Acomo, but I will continue to follow the stock as even a flat EBITDA performance in the next few years could still make this company a ‘buy’.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hub Group: Decent Upside Even Under Conservative Assumptions
data.description:
Investment Overview

I give a buy rating for Hub Group, Inc. (HUBG) because of the visible catalysts, operating leverage, and a decent valuation. Even with conservative assumptions, I am still able to bridge to a decent upside over the next two years.

Business Description

HUBG operates two key segments: Intermodal and Transportation Solutions (ITS) and Logistics. The ITS segment is the asset-heavy business that provides long-haul transport, primarily via intermodal shipping. Currently, HUBG owns a fleet of ~51,000 shipping containers and >2,300 tractors, which they use to handle drayage. Customer-wise, HUBG contracts with major railroads for the long-haul portion and effectively sells that rail capacity in their own service layer. This segment also includes Dedicated Trucking, where HUBG provides specific drivers and equipment exclusively for a single customer's use. As for the logistics segment, this is the asset-light business. Over here, HUBG functions as a broker and manager rather than a physical carrier. This segment includes freight brokerage, where they match shippers with available third-party truck drivers, as well as final-mile delivery for bulky items like appliances, and consolidation services that combine smaller shipments to save money.

The Transcontinental Merger

The pending merger between Union Pacific and Norfolk Southern is a huge catalyst for HUBG. This is not just another rail consolidation; I believe this merger would structurally alter the national freight map, one that benefits HUBG a lot. For context, HUBG is already Union Pacific’s largest intermodal partner and one of the top-5 partners for Norfolk Southern. If this merger goes through, the merged entity will have a true coast-to-coast rail network, which cuts off the need to hand off freight in Chicago or St. Louis. This cutoff is also why long-haul rail was not able to compete effectively vs. long-haul trucking (reduced transit time and cost). With that out of the way, rail would be in a much better position to compete for this long-haul demand.

Since HUBG is already embedded as one of the primary commercial solution providers between shippers and these railroads, HUBG does not need to recalibrate their network and solutions to take advantage of this merger. And since HUBG already has ~30% excess capacity sitting idle in its container fleet today, that means incremental volumes can be absorbed without meaningful capital spending. In other words, revenue growth drops through at very high incremental margins.

The Cycle Will Turn

The US freight cycle has been going through a slump for quite a while, but I believe the cycle will eventually turn. It is inevitable because prolonged low rates will force excess trucking capacity to exit the market as smaller carriers will go bankrupt or let their trucks stay idle. At some point, demand will outstrip supply, and that is when truckload rates will recover. I don’t know when this inflection point will come, but what I do know is that HUBG will benefit a lot because intermodal pricing is a derivative of truckload pricing. When truck rates increase, the spread between road and rail widens, and that forces shippers to migrate volume back to intermodal. This is an inevitable dynamic as well because shippers are largely commoditized businesses; for most operators (except for super-scale ones), they compete aggressively on price, so cost matters.

This would not only benefit HUBG’s topline but also result in massive operational leverage because its cost structure is largely fixed (HUBG owns the containers and employs the drivers regardless of volume). As such, when pricing returns, the incremental revenue flows straight down to earnings. Investors buying today are essentially purchasing a call option on this pricing recovery.

To give a sense of how much margin can expand, I think we can just look at J.B. Hunt’s (JBHT) historical EBIT margin (in the 8 to 10% range). As of Q3 2025, HUBG ITS EBIT margins were ~3%, way below JBHT’s high-single-digits level. If JBHT can achieve that margin, I believe when pricing recovers, HUBG should be able to do so as well. I mean, if you look at HUBG's own financials back in FY22, it managed to achieve an EBIT margin of 9% as well (blended basis). I like that management has taken the time to figure out the internal inefficiencies that the business has today, such as empty miles, imbalanced container flows, and suboptimal routing. These are fixable problems, and if they can fix them, it makes the case for margin to expand even more credible.

Valuation

The better news is that HUBG doesn’t need a roaring freight/trucking market to deliver attractive returns for investors. If you model out HUBG to recover back to FY23 revenue levels over 3 years (really slow recovery) and earnings margins to expand to ~5%, slightly above FY23 levels, HUBG would deliver $210 million of adj. net income (slightly above FY23 levels). This margin assumption also assumes HUBG will not hit the ~10% EBIT margin level that JBHT did historically. On that $210 million, simply assuming a through-cycle multiple of 16x (the historical average), and you get to ~17% upside by the end of 2027. Of course, multiples could stay high like where it is today (22x), and if that happens, the upside becomes a lot better.

Risk

If the Surface Transportation Board blocks the merger deal, the structural catalyst for winning long-haul share will go away, and that could put a dent in the investment thesis. Any prolonged weakness in the current freight cycle would also mean HUBG will take longer than expected to see earnings recover. In the worst case, if the cycle gets worse, HUBG's fixed cost structure could turn against itself.

Conclusion

I am bullish on HUBG because even with conservative assumptions, the stock can deliver solid upside. If the freight cycle turns faster than expected or the rail merger goes through, HUBG could see much stronger growth than I modeled.

data.title: TSMC Strong Buy: The AI Supercycle Is Outrunning Chip Supply
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited or TSMC (TSM) provided its Q4 2025 earnings on January 15th, beating analyst estimates on revenues and earnings. Since my last report, the stock has gained 16.6% outperforming the S&P 500’s (SP500) 3% gain. In this report, I discuss the company’s earnings, the outlook for 2026 and update my price target for the semiconductor giant.

TSMC Puts Efficiency On Display As Capacity Is Constrained

TSM’s sales grew 1.9% sequentially in dollars to $33.73 billion. In NTD growth was 5.7%. Year-on-year, revenues increased 25.5% in USD and 20.5% in NTD. Gross margins were 62.3%, which exceed the guidance and marked sequential as well as year-on-year improvement. I believe the gross margin performance was the highlight in the earnings release. Company has been pointing out that its geographical diversification strategy comes at a margin headwind. That is not just driven by new facilities going through a learning and ramping curve, but by set up and cost structure the full run rate margins of these facilities is below that of the facilities in Taiwan. Operating margins grew 340 basis points sequentially and 500 basis points year-on-year.

Margins primarily were stronger due to better-than-expected cost improvements. We note that volumes declined 3% sequentially, but the company still was able to increase revenues displaying margin and mix strength.

Quarter

7nm

By node, we see that since Q3 2024, the 5nm node revenues held up quite well as a share of sales in the 7nm or below segment. In Q4, that came down to 52%. That is not an indication of weakness, but shows a faster than expected ramp of 3nm node revenues with 3nm margins expected to be accretive to the composite margin in 2026. As demand for 3nm increases further, we will see more 5nm capacity being converted to 3nm capacity driving the 3nm share up even more.

Year-on-year, High Performance Compute grew 48% and now accounts for 58% of the sales. This is where we see the strength of the AI supercycle. Smartphone sales grew 11% while Internet of Things, or IoT, grew 15% and automotive grew 34%. DCE was flat year-over-year as digital consumer electronics demand is pressured. Given the challenges experienced in the automotive industry globally, the 34% growth rate was surprising to me.

Sequentially, we note that DCE declined 22%, automotive was down 1%, Internet of Things was up 3%, smartphones was up 11%, and HPC was up 4%. We note that sequential growth does not point at a cooling in HPC demand, but growth is capped by available wafer supply.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For TSMC?

The main opportunity of course lies in the High Performance Compute demand. At the same time, we note that the ability to supply limits growth. The main limitation right now seems to be packaging. TSM will substantially step up its CapEx from $40.9 billion in 2025 to $52-$56 billion in 2026 with 10%-20% earmarked for advanced packaging. Growth in 2026 primarily is realized through efficiency gains, converting node 5 capacity to node 3 capacity and capital expenditures from prior years coming online. Most of the CapEx spending in 2026 will add capacity in the 2028-2029 frame.

During the year, we will also see node 2 ramping up which will have a 200-300 basis point margin headwind and overseas fabs will also dilute margins. The company is putting more production in other parts of the world, and the positive is that many more countries have a vested interest in TSM being in stable waters, meaning that in the China-Taiwan tension, they may be more vocal.

At the same time, the supply chains and costs outside of Taiwan are less optimized, and so as the company expands internationally, there are some margin headwinds as the overseas fabs have lower margins at full run rate. Additionally, a local-for-local approach may reduce tariff headwinds and provide a tailwind to sales for countries trying to boost the semiconductor industry in their own countries. In some way, we are not seeing a major difference in risks and opportunities, but we are seeing TSM stepping up investments to address bottlenecks that are clearly limiting growth.

TSM Provides Color On Growth

For the first quarter, TSM is guiding for $34.6-$35.8 billion in sales, which points at 4.4% sequential growth at the midpoint. Gross margins are expected to be 63%-64% with operating margins at 54%-56%. This is driven by productivity, node mix and strategic pricing.

For the full year, the company expects 30% growth and provided a 25% CAGR outlook for 2024-2029 with margins of 56% or more. AI accelerator growth is expected to have a compounded annual growth rate of mid to high 50s%.

TSM Strong Buy Rating Maintained

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

We note that TSM is currently valued in line with peers. So, the stock is currently valued in line with 2025 earnings. My price target has increased from $407.86 to $461.27 indicating 35% upside. EBITDA came in 2% below expectations, but has been increased by 9.7% for this year and by 6% for 2027. The total revision is 7.7% with CAGR increasing from 19.8% to 24.6%. On free cash flow, we note that 2025 was substantially stronger than previously anticipated, but estimates for 2026-2027 came down by 2.8% on higher capital expenditures.

Looking at the EBITDA and EBITDA margin performance, we see strong growth in EBITDA but as more overseas capacity comes online and Node 2 ramps up, the margins are expected to contract in 2027. Margin increases in 2026 are primarily driven by higher utilization. Operating cash flow is expected to grow significantly, but on the back of higher capital expenditures to address bottlenecks and support demand from 2028 the free cash flow performance in 2026 will be more flattish. We also see that back in 2026 with a higher share of CapEx against cash flows. It is somewhat unfortunate that TSM does not repurchase shares, because the company is in a net cash position which is expected to hit $122 billion by 2027.

Conclusion: TSMC Growth Remains Impressive

The fourth quarter results show very strong margin execution as the company is seeing a fast ramp up on the 3nm node and higher productivity is also driving margins. For the years ahead there has been a substantial lift to earnings estimates, but we also note that the company is going to spend substantially more on CapEx to address bottlenecks and support demand from 2028 onward. The company is currently limited by capacity rather than any shortage in demand and we note that while the company is expected to grow revenues 30% this year, that growth figure is still limited by capacity. I maintain my strong rating as I consider it an extremely strong sign of market demand that 30% growth is a supply limited growth rate and not a demand limited growth rate.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ONEOK: Buy This Income Machine On Sale Now
data.description:
This article was written by Kody Kester (Kody's Dividends).

In life, there's often a spectrum of opportunities. This is no different than in the world of dividend growth investing. My investing strategy embraces this reality.

Lately, I have found myself leaning a bit more heavily into lower-yielding, more growth-oriented picks. Think Mastercard (MA), Visa (V), and Microsoft (MSFT). Recent middle-ground picks that include a nice mix of both yield and growth include Automatic Data Processing (ADP) and Brookfield Asset Management (BAM).

Finally, I like to include high but sustainable yields with more modest dividend growth prospects. This brings me to the focus of today, which is ONEOK (OKE). Closing out 2025, I added to this pick in three of the last four months (all but November).

When I last covered ONEOK with a Buy rating in August, I was optimistic about acquisition-related synergies. Plans for a major new Permian plant were another positive. The BBB S&P credit rating was a plus as well. Finally, shares were undervalued by a double-digit percentage to my fair value estimate.

Five months later, I'm reaffirming my buy rating. ONEOK has numerous organic growth projects in its pipeline. The company remains on track to deliver synergies to shareholders. ONEOK's deleveraging efforts are progressing. Lastly, shares are slightly more discounted now than they were in August.

ONEOK's Growth Profile Remains Strong

On October 28th, ONEOK shared its earnings report for the third quarter ended September 30th, 2025. The company's revenue soared 71.9% over the year-ago period to $8.63 billion in the quarter. Over that time, the share count only grew at a 7.6% clip, signaling strong revenue growth on a per-share basis as well.

What variables led to this huge topline growth during the third quarter? Like my prior article, acquisitions were the major driver of this growth for the quarter.

The $3.3 billion acquisition of a 43% stake in Global Infrastructure Partners' EnLink Midstream, completed last August, was a key acquisition. Months later, ONEOK closed on a stock deal to acquire the remaining 57% stake in the company.

In August 2024, the midstream operator also completed a $2.6 billion acquisition of Medallion Midstream. This deal for the largest crude gathering and transportation system in the Permian's Midland Basin was acquired for a 2025 P/EBITDA ratio of 6.3x (including base case synergies).

Just as I noted in August, ONEOK finished the last several weeks of the second quarter by acquiring the remaining 49.9% minority interest in Delaware LLC from NGP XI Midstream Holdings for $940 million ($530 million in cash and $410 million in stock). Now, the company owns the entirety of over 700 million cubic feet per day of processing capacity of these facilities in the Delaware Basin.

Natural gas processed volumes more than doubled to 5.85 billion cubic feet per day over Q3 2024. Incremental volumes from EnLink and increased production in the Mid-Continent and Rocky Mountain regions were to credit.

Sequentially, ONEOK's most recent acquisition and investments helped its Gulf Coast/Permian region NGL volumes rise by 8%. Rocky Mountain region NGL volumes and total natural gas volumes processed both increased by 5% over Q2 2025.

ONEOK's adjusted EBITDA jumped 37.2% year-over-year to $2.12 billion in the third quarter. Sequentially, the metric grew by 7%.

ONEOK continues to have a promising future ahead. This is evidenced by a variety of organic growth projects under construction or in the pipeline.

The refined products expansion to the Denver area will increase system capacity by 35,000 barrels per day. That's expected to be completed in the middle of this year.

The first phase of a project to rebuild the 210,000-barrel-per-day fractionator in Medford, Oklahoma, will be complete by the end of 2026 (100,000 barrels per day). In Q1 2027, the other 110,000 barrels per day of capacity will come online.

Then, there's the Bighorn CO2 gas processing plant, expected to cost $365 million. Construction is set to begin later this year, with the asset slated to be placed into service by mid-year 2027.

One of the more noteworthy projects in the more distant future is the Eiger Express Pipeline joint venture with WhiteWater and Enbridge (ENB). While construction isn't yet underway, the timeline for this to come online remains mid-year 2028.

Another piece of the growth puzzle for ONEOK has to do with its synergies. In his opening remarks during the Q3 2025 Earnings Call, President and CEO Pierce Norton noted that the company was on track to realize $250 million of incremental synergies in 2025. With meaningful synergies across all of its recent acquisitions under its control, ONEOK isn't just becoming a bigger enterprise. It's becoming more efficient, with additional synergies likely to be unlocked in 2026.

The final piece of growth for ONEOK comes via its capital allocation policy. CFO Walter Hulse expects that the company will approach its long-term leverage target of 3.5x in Q4 2026. Upon completing its deleveraging, this will further solidify ONEOK's BBB S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

That will free up additional capital for the company to fulfill more of its $2 billion share repurchase authorization. We're already seeing this to a modest degree, with $45 million of buybacks completed in Q3 2025.

For these reasons, the analyst consensus is that operating cash flow per share will increase by 9.3% in 2025 to $9.21. For 2026, another 17.1% surge in OCF per share to $10.78 is anticipated. In 2027, an additional 6.5% increase in OCF per share to $11.48 is projected (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to ONEOK's Q3 2025 Earnings Press Release, ONEOK's Q3 2025 10-Q Filing, ONEOK's Q3 2025 Earnings Presentation, and ONEOK's January 2026 Investor Presentation).

Fair Value Is Approaching $95 A Share

In the last five months, OKE's shares have posted a 5% total return. That somewhat lagged the S&P 500 index's (SP500) 9% total return logged over that time.

The uptick in the share price has lagged the pace at which its operating cash flow per share input has grown. This means that despite the recent rally, shares are arguably cheaper now than they were five months ago.

To my point, OKE is trading at a forward 12-month P/OCF ratio of 7. That's slightly lower than the previous value of 7.1. For more perspective, this is far below the 10-year average P/OCF ratio of 9.7, per FAST Graphs.

As was the case in August, I am standing by my fair value P/OCF ratio of 8.7. What's my rationale behind this decision?

OKE's annual OCF per share growth consensus in the high single-digit range (adjusting for the lone estimate for 2028 projecting a modest decline) is comparable to the average CAGR in recent years of 6.5%. That implies its growth potential is intact.

What's more, my fair value multiple of 8.7 would also be perfectly in line with the 20-year average P/OCF ratio. Given the growth driven by data centers, LNG, and ammonia facilities, I believe that such a P/OCF ratio can be defended.

As of today (January 14th, 2026), the calendar year 2026 is 4% behind us. This suggests there is another 96% of 2026 and 4% of 2027 still ahead over the coming 12 months. That yields a forward 12-month OCF per share input of $10.81.

Plugging this into my fair value P/OCF ratio of 8.7, I compute a fair value of $94 a share. Against the current $76 share price, this signals shares are priced at a 20% discount to my fair value estimate.

If OKE reverts to fair value and matches growth projections, it could deliver a 33% total return in 2026. Through 2030, OKE could produce 14% annual total returns.

The Payout Has Room To Grow

OKE's 5.4% forward dividend yield comes in well above the energy sector's 3.8% median forward yield. This is lower than all of its MLP counterparts. On the other hand, it's higher than C-Corp counterparts, such as Kinder Morgan (KMI) and The Williams Companies (WMB). Additionally, OKE's spread has grown from 100 basis points to the sector median in August to 160 basis points now. That's why Seeking Alpha's Quant System awards respective B and B- grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield (up from B- and C+ grades in August, respectively).

OKE's solid starting income is secure, too. The company's payout ratio is set to be in the low-70% range for 2026, which is below the 83% payout ratio that rating agencies desire for the midstream industry. This strikes a balance between rewarding shareholders with immediate income and paving the way for payout boosts.

As a result, the Zen Research Terminal anticipates a 0.7% risk of a dividend cut over the next 12 months. In the next average recession, the probability of a payout reduction remains muted at 2.5%. That's why I believe the C+ grade from the Quant System is about a letter grade too conservative (down from a B grade in August, despite the payout ratio improving from the mid-70% range in 2025 to the low-70% range for 2026).

OKE has grown its dividend for 3 consecutive years (after temporarily freezing it during the COVID-19 pandemic). This is better than the sector median of 2.1 years. That accounts for the Quant System issuing a B grade for overall dividend consistency (unchanged from August).

Finally, OKE appears positioned to extend its dividend growth streak with mid-single-digit annual percentage payout raises. The Quant System forecasts a 4.1% annual forward dividend per share growth rate, which is reasonable in my view. That would be a bit below the sector median of 4.8%. This explains the respective C and B+ grades from the Quant System for the forward dividend per share growth metric and overall dividend growth (the latter of which is up from a B grade in August).

Risks To Consider

ONEOK is a company with an encouraging growth outlook. However, some risks could alter or even break the investment thesis.

One of the most notable risks facing ONEOK remains commodity price volatility. But aren't approximately 90% of the company's 2025 earnings set to be generated from fee-based sources?

Yes, that's true. Still, there are a couple of ways that ONEOK is indirectly still vulnerable to commodity price volatility. For one, beaten-down commodity prices could pressure the financial positioning of the company's less robust customers. That could cause some of ONEOK's customers to default on their financial obligations, which could weigh on its financial results.

The longer-term implication from such an instance would be reduced investment in exploration and production by the midstream operator's customers. This could put pressure on ONEOK's growth potential.

Another risk to the company is the potential that its key growth projects could encounter cost overruns or delays resulting from legal opposition. If that happens, it could also hurt ONEOK's growth story.

One final risk to the company stems from its status as one of the continent's largest midstream operators. ONEOK's vital energy infrastructure makes it a target for attempted cyber breaches. If any are successful, this could result in customer lawsuits against the company and fines from regulators.

Summary: A SWAN Pick In My Portfolio

Comprising 2.4% of my portfolio, OKE is my 10th-biggest investment holding. The company's variety of high-return organic growth projects and synergies from recent acquisitions provide it with clear growth catalysts. OKE also projects that it will approach its long-term leverage target in Q4 of this year. The company's market-beating dividend is well-covered and has the flexibility to keep rising.

Finally, ONEOK is an undeniable bargain. Shares are valued 20% below my fair value estimate. That provides a path to 30%+ upside in 2026 and 14% annual total returns through 2030. In closing, I'm sticking with my Buy rating.

data.title: I Expect AMD's HBM Focus Will Steal Market Share Away From Nvidia
data.description:
Over the last few years, I’ve covered Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), three times here on Seeking Alpha.

While I initially thought shares looked expensive (and somewhat risky) in mid-2024, I upgraded shares to a Buy - and then reiterated that rating - in the winter and spring of 2025. Since those two Buy ratings, shares of AMD are up roughly 100%, significantly outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500) and validating my core thesis - that AMD’s recent shift toward focusing primarily on AI chips would position it to compete more effectively with Nvidia (NVDA):

Fast forward to the present, and we’re seeing the continued fruits of AMD’s labor.

Since focusing more heavily on AI, the company has been hard at work developing its next-generation chipsets, which were announced recently at the Consumer Electronics Show in Las Vegas.

Announced on the same day as Nvidia’s next-generation Rubin architecture, AMD’s launch stood out because management chose to go in a different direction than the market leader Nvidia, focusing on high-bandwidth memory rather than pure, raw computing power.

While Nvidia’s next-gen chips are still expected to outperform AMD’s on speed, I expect that AMD’s new chips, which feature considerably more local HBM capacity, could widen AMD’s potential customer base and provide a more compelling alternative to Nvidia versus last year.

Combined with a stock whose overall growth, profitability, and valuation add up to significant appreciation potential, I am reiterating my Buy rating on the company.

Today, I'll break down AMD’s recent announcements, explain the impact they could have on the firm's financials, and make the case that shares of AMD are still a buy, even after the recent rally.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

It’s been roughly eight months since I last covered AMD, and in that time the company has turned in a number of strong quarterly earnings reports. In Q2 and Q3 of 2025, AMD reported earnings per share of $0.48 and $1.20, respectively.

On the revenue side, AMD reported revenues of $7.69 billion and $9.25 billion, also representing beats across both the top and bottom lines versus analyst estimates. Of particular note was AMD’s Q3 revenue number, which came in at $9.3 billion, up 36% year over year and marking a clear all-time high for the company:

As revenue has accelerated from the company’s growth slump throughout 2023 and 2024, shares of the company have found new life in the AI era.

Additionally, these recent earnings reports show just how much AMD’s focus on the AI chip market is paying off. While client and gaming revenues were actually up more than data center revenue YoY, the cyclicality of those industries has been buffered by recent advances AMD has made in the cloud space.

Now, with the announcement of AMD's new chips, I expect the company's next leg higher, revenue-wise, will once again come from the data center segment.

For context, in the first week of January, Nvidia and AMD both introduced their next-generation chipsets, respectively called Vera Rubin, and Helios.

These are not consumer chipsets. They are primarily targeted toward the institutional cloud market, which has made up a huge percentage of overall compute spend in recent years as the AI buildout has continued apace.

As expected, on a compute-speed basis, Nvidia’s total Rubin chipsets are still faster than AMD’s, clocking in at roughly 50 petaflops on FP4 versus 40 petaflops on FP4 for AMD.

This performance difference of 20% to 25% matters, especially when you are trying to quickly ship new foundational models. As a result, I expect Nvidia will continue to see the lion’s share of data center chip demand over the next year.

That said, the environment is changing as AI continues to mature.

On the development side, there are two types of workloads: training and inference. Training workloads require high-speed compute above all else, as building new models requires enormous amounts of data. However, inference workloads, which essentially refer to when users force models to "think," make up a huge percentage of all tasks, and will continue to grow rapidly as user adoption increases.

These inference workloads are typically less computationally intensive, (FP4 or 8 versus FP16+), and far more memory-intensive, which helps explain the recent surge in memory pricing and memory-related stocks like Micron Technologies:

As a result, memory is playing a larger role in overall system performance than ever before.

AMD appears to recognize this, which is why its new chips feature roughly 31 TB of HBM4 per rack, compared to approximately 21 TB of HBM4 for Nvidia's new Rubin systems.

Nvidia, for their part, expects they will be able to run their smaller memory configurations at higher exaflops, helping offset some of this difference. That said, I anticipate that AMD’s higher local memory capacity will make its chips more competitive, particularly for inference workloads in big LLM models. Not only that, but lower memory FLOPs also mean lower power consumption and a lower TCO.

As a result, I am expecting an acceleration in AMD’s year-over-year data center revenue growth to above the 30% mark.

Combined with recent strength in the gaming and client segments, AMD appears to be firing on all cylinders.

Analysts currently anticipate year-over-year revenue growth of roughly 26% and year-over-year earnings growth of about 25%, which seems reasonable to me:

If AMD’s advanced chipsets can push those numbers even higher as its customer base widens, we could be looking at a massive year for the company.

If AMD can sustain growth in the 30% plus range - even as absolute revenue scales meaningfully - there is considerable room for organic shareholder returns.

Valuation

On a valuation basis, I see shares of AMD as particularly well priced at the present moment.

Not only are the company’s segments performing well, but there is a considerable market share opportunity if AMD’s new chips achieve the specifications CEO Lisa Su presented at CES. If that execution materializes, the stock looks like a compelling buy.

Currently, AMD trades at a forward price-to-earnings-to-growth ratio, or PEG, of roughly 1.2, representing about a 26% discount to the sector and only an 8% premium to the company’s average valuation multiple over the last five years:

This implies that on a forward earnings growth basis, AMD is cheaper than peers and roughly in line with its own historical valuation. This also shows that the recent rally in AMD shares is not purely speculative, but instead reflects materially improving financial prospects on the part of the company.

On a trailing basis, AMD does still look expensive, with a price-to-sales ratio north of 12x and a trailing price-to-earnings ratio around 117x. There is no question that in 2025, AMD has been a more expensive stock than the broader market.

That said, analysts expect earnings growth of roughly 65%, 56%, and 38% YoY between 2026 and 2028, which could compress AMD’s price-to-earnings ratio from roughly 34, to 22, and eventually 16 over the next three years:

I believe this growth trajectory represents a meaningful opportunity for investors.

Either AMD shares will trade sideways while the price gets cheaper and cheaper, or earnings growth translates more directly into share price appreciation. In either case, the setup appears enticing.

As a result, I am reiterating my Buy rating on AMD.

Risks

The risks here are threefold.

First, AMD must execute on the delivery of its new MI400-series chips. Any delays, technical issues, or manufacturing problems could materially dampen the company’s 2026 growth outlook.

Second, competition from Nvidia remains a primary concern. Nvidia’s chips have historically delivered a lower total cost of operations, and the company has maintained its dominant position throughout the AI boom. While higher local HBM capacity and the move to more advanced process nodes (2nm) could improve AMD’s power efficiency, it is possible that AMD’s strategic direction proves less effective than expected.

Finally, there is valuation risk. While AMD appears reasonably priced relative to its growth and historical multiples, the stock still trades at more than 100x trailing earnings.

In the event of a broader market correction, high-multiple stocks tend to sell off first, and as a result, macro volatility could translate into elevated short-term volatility in AMD’s share price.

Summary

Overall, while risks remain, I view AMD’s recent chip launches and increased emphasis on high-bandwidth memory as a net positive.

If AMD can continue to take market share from Nvidia, the company has a significant growth opportunity ahead.

Trading at only a modest premium to its own historical valuation and positioned for strong future growth, I believe shares of AMD represent a buy opportunity.

Cheers~
